# Google Buys Compute From SpaceX, Broadcom’s Outlook, Apple’s AI Politics (Stratechery Update 6-8-2026)

**From:** Ben Thompson <email@stratechery.com>  
**Date:** 2026-06-08T10:01:58.000Z

---

Google's deal with SpaceX, and Broadcom's earnings, both seem bullish for Nvidia. Then, what I'm looking for at WWDC.

View in browser ( https://stratechery.com/2026/google-buys-compute-from-spacex-broadcoms-outlook-apples-ai-politics/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..WIx3s68YNaZRP6p8QvqUmV905V77_kwXvfSIYGPlCwPyGJ10Q35ZOM-weIBBUxxxL87SLPctOsyu3S0M3vHN2Y23Q_f6eqn0j-HVzwK5pwO0-W67qgywuh3UpqVM42BYQj-mwAfS4dMSQElwT9AFxezQuK_Hpw325LCGPdIf1FyY5TqQaFTRpN0_tnBP_lY9yhfImsC7EiljNLYVjn2HHgURAe81k189is-Z2KzBAfS_Q3mDU6JcsOQk6AYWBlds7wHXoKvmyO-dDqdYM6gCEdUUDLvjYU2oJQzhYCONeBrbu-DF-_rLCKXa-rC_lQdiKbdqF6rqvb7k8Qt2Ez2yPw ) | Listen in your podcast player ( https://stratechery.passport.online/member/podcast?url=https%3A%2F%2Frss.stratechery.passport.online%2Ffeed%2Fpodcast%2FCLKjCzLJnXsHRUFPqZ2K9 )

Google Buys Compute From SpaceX, Broadcom’s Outlook, Apple’s AI Politics
************************************************************************

( https://stratechery.com/2026/google-buys-compute-from-spacex-broadcoms-outlook-apples-ai-politics/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..WIx3s68YNaZRP6p8QvqUmV905V77_kwXvfSIYGPlCwPyGJ10Q35ZOM-weIBBUxxxL87SLPctOsyu3S0M3vHN2Y23Q_f6eqn0j-HVzwK5pwO0-W67qgywuh3UpqVM42BYQj-mwAfS4dMSQElwT9AFxezQuK_Hpw325LCGPdIf1FyY5TqQaFTRpN0_tnBP_lY9yhfImsC7EiljNLYVjn2HHgURAe81k189is-Z2KzBAfS_Q3mDU6JcsOQk6AYWBlds7wHXoKvmyO-dDqdYM6gCEdUUDLvjYU2oJQzhYCONeBrbu-DF-_rLCKXa-rC_lQdiKbdqF6rqvb7k8Qt2Ez2yPw ) Monday, June 8, 2026

Good morning,

On Thursday’s episode of Sharp Tech ( https://sharptech.fm/member/episode/whats-google-doing-with-berkshire-hathaway-a-bubble-temperature-check-gen-z-you-tubers-take-hollywood ) , Andrew and I discussed Google’s deal with Berkshire Hathaway, and YouTubers and Hollywood.

On to the Update:

Google Buys Compute From SpaceX
-------------------------------

From Bloomberg ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-05/google-buying-computing-from-spacex-in-920-million-a-month-deal ) :

Alphabet Inc.’s Google has agreed to pay Elon Musk’s SpaceX $920 million a month for computing power as part of a cloud services deal that runs through mid-2029, its second such agreement with an AI competitor in a matter of weeks. Google will pay SpaceX the monthly fee from October this year through June 2029, SpaceX said in the filing Friday. That amounts to about $30 billion through the time of the agreement. If SpaceX fails to deliver access to Nvidia Corp. chips as part of the deal by Sept. 30, Google has the right to terminate the contract, with a one-month grace period, the filing shows.

A Google Cloud spokesperson said the deal would help the company meet demand for its AI services. In its most recent earnings report, Alphabet said Google Cloud’s backlog — the measure of contracted work that hasn’t been recorded as revenue yet — nearly doubled from the prior quarter to more than $460 billion. “This is a short-term, timely agreement to ensure we have bridge capacity to meet surging customer demand for our agent platform, Gemini Enterprise, which has been even higher than we expected,” the Google spokesperson said in a statement…

Under the pact disclosed on Friday, either party also has the right to terminate the arrangement with 90 days’ notice — the same feature as in Anthropic’s deal.

When I wrote about SpaceX a couple of weeks ago ( https://stratechery.com/2026/the-spacex-ipo-and-data-centers-in-space/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..csax0pMliweljNIyQZfPLfCGuRFeAcFLDPIc_2onQn45BO5sYUHKI1FpbO72LDY--dwOI-JWvQwSsp_bBjT1PsdB84tLFOQ_6o6iOyRgUXwDdC27utXRyTD0dRqIr4DlvDV_mMY_oZVaYgzNFNrrijr8pqpE7y9NSZhM7seLiLysMgtbR8UFjXrW3QcOP6lYfOzjhlc7C3BrlnXyTPEkso_uY57wXEK9nYPZyQUmOqQhBV3k2181rp5kDaak4zQJvzYH3Hiz_p1aC9PeZYMSwhUEWztT9IeV4ZFnvqUQ9L3VdPGF1jon-e89ymT-Ehuy1tr-bxiVV8IH_7ymR-zwvg ) , several people asked why I didn’t more heavily weigh the company’s deal with Anthropic in my analysis, and the reason is that Musk himself said not to, emphasizing on X that the deal was short-term and subject to be canceled at any time.

SpaceX is, by all accounts, moving forward with its Cursor not-an-acquisition-until-after-the-IPO deal ( https://stratechery.com/2026/john-ternus-and-apples-hardware-defined-future-spacexai-and-cursor/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..c9AMuPLicRLYm--l-NRan7vVQMSLxhMqwgc5ARhxbpIfv2IlWbKMsFb7ePDGsG83Aw66I4Jitv9UAFVj3NeFWX0Hu0l4_hI0SEve3GNZyTHYXNfc78sz_W8DHa6tw7YeTNKsgaSgmGIMSIXoh8cyYt-hcvj9ZxfsETLUz1yoET8UhCFZnj4_cpct_W4qa7OFrBnwzXxL1IuN3LJNHRLlomYIAQkU6wa4dYL0I0XOP-bEav1T4EyjXfm3EHIC9Tso4dzsL7TxbwUVzekeLwCJEiswdw8jvi4quCZSNwyISuTuSpN5KrV6YGyQ2vPQ_4npUSJdKQJ_AVIbXbMYx8rxKg ) ; the Occam’s Razor explanation for all of this activity is that SpaceX has GPUs now that are doing nothing but depreciating — they’re clearly not being used for Grok — but that Musk wants to reserve the right to take GPU capacity back if Cursor is able to train a new model that is a big hit. It’s a reasonable bet, even if — as I wrote after the Anthropic deal ( https://stratechery.com/2026/spacex-and-anthropic-xais-two-companies-elon-musk-and-spacexais-future/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..cAN0T-UrKiJ4DjBZ2ISAT4t_JTCJYGDhIUq6G1YmcXLhPxIufJv7erEvbgBaWOk-QTaadVOfHf9Po0LsqJGFlwBpbDCgpAEoweP3hqj5xrfaPuYLVmkwrQQyWQRNKlxbGc4LuOWv4_Fi4ED5SCNUFlrtQ-zvIDOImUIDZJSGtOHp8hha4sXXCqDkEVGSD9bsCy3Iid0OVW3uHxpzZQ5xr96DCT13OCPxbtc2l4FQ9ZKSqbegRtyMIVjJQCIwGPfVw-cgFCrdyOeBI4bCtg3RLMdQX-HpKnry5sVAG72REGCzlTDmwEDIk0E0JDPeRRZn9M6gZEd_BXEYvP50jeYyqA ) — there is a good case for SpaceX to simply focus on being a neocloud. One obvious benefit is that they would get more credit for being a profitable company not just now — at least once this deal kicks in — but also going forward, but I understand the drive to integrate that capacity with a model in the pursuit of higher margins in the long run.

Regardless, the benefit to SpaceX from this deal is obvious; what is less clear is the payoff for Google. That, naturally, has led to conspiracy theories about Google doing whatever they can to increase the value of their SpaceX investment, and, well, sometimes conspiracy theories are true. One interesting wrinkle is that whereas SpaceX has gotten special exemptions to be included in some stock indexes early, the S&P 500 requires profitability ( https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/05/spacex-blocked-from-early-us-benchmark-index-entry-as-sp-reaffirms-existing-rules.html ) and won’t grant an exception, which this deal helpfully provides, making Google’s share easier to sell at a high price. It’s definitely possible that this is the motivation, but call me an idealist: I don’t think that Google is primarily motivated by investment returns, so I give less weight to this thesis.

What seems just as plausible to me is that a huge part of that Google Cloud backlog actually just wants access to Nvidia chips specifically, not TPUs. Obviously Anthropic is running on TPUs, and was likely a major driver of GCP’s explosive growth last quarter ( https://stratechery.com/2026/google-earnings-meta-earnings/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..i3-9sLt2I-6ZWP6g8Ehi_bckqX_9jFh6QhTZp3Il0wCODC7iawNCrzT_Bj1B99j9mLsCCV33cfoWZ0g_iNw9EeWbfZEpxQFkc4Esplpzy9n0-qlM3OzRca6BmM1JyDxVZlV5MAVcQEwuOp1M2Xkfd8NwrVE1i0z2DGgCvGoJpuvtap4UMI8lRsbQgOsyRIoVuxvurOOclYD7Vceh3Y6w5ojULKtArfQIW3eMVOmcj7Ln9eeJNpAxlC6VGDyDbYR4iqHc1w7sm3l3gQ-CRQMjpJh2R--XlFB0begVieA5C62mN_7zHanYGceHk_xOrZM-sytpxmdBDXeJB5BATl_cmw ) ; we also know that Google is selling TPUs to Anthropic in the future ( https://stratechery.com/2026/anthropics-new-tpu-deal-anthropics-computing-crunch-the-anthropic-google-alliance/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..cyj18ioOHBW7k3JPEhrUr5OnzWb8ln_N-6_PJD0WJeU2lf6_RNNs3SJ-2XAGmxc6fNB36rlGLuWhHLcWM1EDe21wou8V4BmL22Jb82fPEUMdwuIAdZygGt9TrNqNJQ9GJzPPb-YqDjsL3ph5Rxw0ayUx0V-2y1mDwfSd23pjL4bsunLqCmhdh4Z-FaD-NtitAmg6HpbaEBXYTbJmoRHMyW-YWQ9C8om87K1kkoecKXGAcg64Umt0HO-aYmh9h-czwop8Ij49D5Q1oKYXLYkULmugwibu8_ttv2YgqjG9omuormdqVfH5fWvfLWnKHipFGT848Lrst3j2a0VCDaDAzg ). Why would Google do that if it is so starved for capacity? Sure, Anthropic is willing to pay ( https://stratechery.com/2026/mythos-muse-and-the-opportunity-cost-of-compute/?access_token=eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6InN0cmF0ZWNoZXJ5LnBhc3Nwb3J0Lm9ubGluZSIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..j4UU08lNlFyFfQ1C9Z18CSDwDX7ttpDxWF-Vejpo1SrUma1lMEQIqrICxPrgAopnkreL9CBcnAXqKcY8FZHdJKaOLmCfz4XE2TN9RaRh7QF3SDn5PvR4fHv4Vbhl6-dTrbRqpYvrOg4HLyJbNIyCS0lUI7z-lG_GM_HGpFdrKKMjwDsycBjPQVib2bhlZPzsyTc7aePKu6oZ7-t1GZk0QmHjGBE5KKSSRpSlD1C8Vx8EmpIacx4PmUpOw7cN9tsO9gnvqy2LC1i9MX_YDgJcV3xc0BWvG9qfMYO7nQdYFnvJbjWqGM1Rv_xPIRGRWXuTaWITLTS5yISsox9fTsgy5w ) , but any compute relationship with Anthropic is going to be fundamentally transactional; I would think that the lifetime value of selling compute to other customers who want to standardize on Google’s infrastructure would be more important to Google. Perhaps the answer is that everyone-but-Anthropic wants Nvidia chips, not TPUs? Or, to put it another way, this news is the second bit of news that argues for that thesis.

There’s one more angle to consider: the short-term nature of this deal could mean it is just a bridge until Google gets more Nvidia GPUs; it could also mean that Google actually wants to use this cluster for training. There are architectural reasons why Nvidia GPUs — particularly their scale-up networking capabilities — are better for training Mixture-of-Expert models than TPUs. It’s not out of the question that Google just acquired the capacity for training the next version of Gemini, which, definitionally, is capacity it only needs temporarily.

Broadcom’s Outlook
------------------

From Bloomberg ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/broadcom-outlook-disappoints-investors-seeking-ai-fueled-blowout ) :

Broadcom Inc. shares plunged by the most in more than 16 months after the company’s forecast for sales of its artificial intelligence chips disappointed investors. AI semiconductor revenue will be $16 billion in the fiscal third quarter running through July, the company said in a statement Wednesday, falling well below analysts’ expectations of $17.2 billion on average. Chief Executive Officer Hock Tan said Broadcom will sell $56 billion worth of AI chips in the fiscal year that ends in October, also falling short of estimates.

Broadcom’s outlook miss led to a widespread sell-off that has continued in Asian markets overnight. From Reuters on Friday ( https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/chip-selloff-erases-over-1-trillion-stock-market-value-2026-06-05/ ) :

U.S.-traded chipmakers plunged on Friday, losing about $1.3 trillion in market value, with deep losses in AI heavy hitters including Nvidia, Micron Technology, and Advanced Micro Devices, as Broadcom’s weak report earlier this week reverberated across Wall Street. The PHLX chip index slumped 10.3% in its deepest one-day loss since March 2020, when the coronavirus pandemic threw global markets into a tailspin. Friday’s selloff added to losses on Thursday after Broadcom issued a quarterly report that showed demand for its custom AI chips business falling short of lofty expectations.

There wasn’t anything too notable about the Broadcom earnings call ( https://seekingalpha.com/article/4911686-broadcom-inc-avgo-q2-2026-earnings-call-transcript ) (well, beyond CEO Hock Tan accidentally reading last year’s numbers at the beginning). Tan did acknowledge that while Google had signed a new deal for chip development ( https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/broadcom-to-supply-ai-chips-to-google-computing-capacity-to-anthropic-in-expanded-collaboration-c838e1b8 ) , Broadcom wouldn’t be their sole partner:

Now we also accept the fact that while we like to win every design in that program, we also accept the fact that given the growth of consumption and — development and consumption of AI compute even by our partner, Google, that we fully expect that there will be some diversity of sources for them.

Google is already known to be partnering with MediaTek, and is also looking to bring more design capabilities in house; Anthropic, meanwhile, is certainly motivated to do the same in the very long run.

To that end, I do wonder how much this news item is related to the previous news item? Broadcom’s most important AI business is TPUs; is it possible that TPUs are simply well on their way to being commoditized by virtue of having a limited customer base (Google and Anthropic), and that Nvidia is the only choice for basically everyone else? In other words, is Broadcom’s “miss” an AI miss, or an Nvidia win? Absent any other signals — which we probably won’t get until the next earnings cycle — I’m inclined to think it’s the latter.

Apple’s AI Politics
-------------------

From Bloomberg ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/newsletters/2026-06-07/wwdc-2026-apple-s-secret-meeting-that-led-it-to-take-ai-seriously-ios-27 ) :

Around early 2025, Apple Inc. executives held a consequential meeting in a conference room near the software engineering department overseen by Craig Federighi. The assembled leaders included the company’s senior vice presidents, chief operating officer, chief financial officer and others who reported to Chief Executive Officer Tim Cook. Alan Dye, Apple’s former interface design chief, and Mike Rockwell, the creator of the Vision Pro headset, were also present. Cook himself wasn’t there, and Jeff Williams, the company’s now-retired COO, called the meeting to order. The discussion centered on what had by then become a growing crisis: Apple Intelligence, the company’s new AI platform, was a flop, and a critical overhaul of the Siri digital assistant was on the verge of being delayed.

At the same time, rivals such as Meta Platforms Inc., Microsoft Corp., Alphabet Inc.’s Google, OpenAI and Anthropic PBC were rapidly advancing. During the meeting, executives discussed just how much trouble the company was in if real changes weren’t made immediately. (Weeks later, top executive Eddy Cue even publicly said that AI could upend the iPhone business within a decade.)

The purpose of the meeting quickly became to formulate a recommendation to Cook about how the company should respond. By that point, Cook had largely lost confidence in then-AI chief John Giannandrea, who was also in the room. Cook and several executives close to him had concluded that Apple had serious cultural, structural and leadership problems when it came to AI. Federighi led much of the discussion, but Rockwell emerged as a powerful voice. Fresh off the launch of the Vision Pro headset — viewed internally as a technical achievement despite being a sales flop — Rockwell carried significant credibility on major technology initiatives. Passionate about AI, he volunteered during the meeting to become Apple’s fixer for artificial intelligence and Siri.

The whole article from Mark Gurman is worth a read: AI appears, within Apple, to be an excuse for politics and palace intrigue; the ultimate takeaway is that the same executives who didn’t even know generative AI existed until ChatGPT are in charge of incorporating it into Apple’s products.

Of course that may be fine, optimal even: Apple isn’t spending anything close to the money its rivals are on infrastructure, and everyone is going to need devices to access AI. Still, Apple needs some sort of software story, whether that be actually making Siri functional, or more ideally (yet also more unlikely), opening up its operating system to other companies’ agentic capabilities.

The answer to these questions should be unveiled later today in the WWDC keynote. There are three big questions I am most interested in, not just for their own sake, but also for what they say about how Apple is approaching AI:

* First, will Apple announce a means for vibe-coded services to deploy apps on a user’s iPhone without having to go through the App Store?
* Second, will Apple open up Siri App Intents to other AI services, so that those services can interact with other apps on the iPhone?
* Third, will Apple announce any special provisions in macOS for agentic services like OpenClaw?

All of these questions come down to the tradeoff between control and capability. Apple has for years prioritized the former, but AI is so compelling that not giving ground to the latter actually runs a real risk of users considering something else. I think Apple would do well to signal that, while they will offer AI experiences, they also won’t stand in the way of users who want more. That’s the best way to keep their devices relevant in the long run.

This Update will be available as a podcast later today. To receive it in your podcast player, visit Stratechery ( https://stratechery.passport.online/member ).

Thanks for being a subscriber, and have a great day!
